headline: Lal Baum Born, 1937
body:
It was a curious turn of the wheel, indeed, that on the fourteenth day of October, in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and thirty-seven, a lad named Laurence George Baum, though he would be known to the world as Lal, first drew breath in the rugged, silver-laced earth of Silver Bow, Montana. Now, Silver Bow, mind you, was no Emerald City, nor was it a place where yellow brick roads were laid with any regularity. It was a place of hard rock and harder men, a far cry from the fantastical realms conjured by his own great-grandfather, the celebrated L. Frank Baum, who had, with a mere flick of his pen, whisked a Kansas girl away to Oz. One might say young Lal was born with a certain legacy, a shadow, perhaps, of glittering slippers and talking scarecrows, though his path would lead him not to magical kingdoms, but to the flickering lights of the silver screen. 

His early years in Montana, one can imagine, were steeped in the kind of honest, unvarnished reality that would have delighted a keen observer of human nature. The vast skies, the whisper of the wind through the pines, the distant rumble of trains carrying the fruits of the earth – these were his childhood companions, a stark contrast to the make-believe worlds that were, in a manner of speaking, his birthright. Yet, the call of the stage, or rather, the camera, proved irresistible. He journeyed westward, as many a hopeful soul did, to that peculiar land of dreams and disillusionment, Hollywood, where fortunes were made and unmade with the swiftness of a Montana summer storm. 

Lal Baum, the actor, carved out a respectable, if not star-studded, career. He appeared in such moving pictures as *Chandler* in seventy-one, and graced the small screen in popular programs like *The Immortal* in sixty-nine and *The Rockford Files* in seventy-four. He was a man who understood the craft, the subtle art of pretending, of inhabiting another's skin for the brief span of a scene. He married Tisha Sterling, herself a player in that grand theatrical enterprise, and together they brought forth a daughter, Heidi Kristen Baum, adding another branch to that storied family tree. 

One cannot help but ponder the thoughts of his illustrious ancestor, L. Frank Baum, on such a life. The elder Baum, a man who understood the power of the mind to shape reality, once declared, “I believe that dreams - day dreams, you know, with your eyes wide open and your brain machinery whizzing - are likely to lead to the betterment of the world.” One wonders if Lal, in his quiet moments, reflected on this, on the dreams he chased under the California sun, so different, yet perhaps so akin in spirit, to the grand visions of Oz. His was a life lived in the shadow of a literary giant, yet he forged his own path, a Montana man who sought his fortune not in gold or silver, but in the ephemeral glow of the moving image. He passed from this mortal coil on July twenty-first, nineteen eighty-seven, in Los Angeles, a victim of a cancerous brain tumor, leaving behind a legacy of performances and the quiet dignity of a man who lived his own dream, however modest it might have seemed against the backdrop of his family's fantastical heritage.
